content.text: A GenAI ROI model is not a spreadsheet exercise that justifies a decision already made — it is the primary tool for deciding whether to fund a use case, at what scale, and on what timeline. CFOs and programme sponsors who have been burned by over-optimistic ROI models from prior digital transformation waves apply higher scrutiny to AI investment cases. The ROI model must be structured, independently verifiable, and conservative by default. It must show the bear case with the same prominence as the base case.

attrs.alt: GenAI ROI model structure: Benefits minus Costs drives Output Metrics

content.text: Why GenAI ROI Is Different from Standard Software ROI

Standard software ROI models assume deterministic outcomes: a new ERP system processes 100% of transactions with a defined error rate. GenAI ROI models carry three sources of uncertainty that standard software models do not:

— The AI model will be correct N% of the time. Benefits that depend on automated decisions (handling rate, error reduction) are a function of accuracy that cannot be precisely known before production. A 5% drop in accuracy can halve the projected benefit.

— Unlike a mandatory ERP, most GenAI systems are optional augmentation tools. If only 60% of eligible staff use the system, 40% of the projected benefit evaporates. Adoption is the most commonly undermodelled variable in GenAI ROI.

— The cost of running the AI (API calls, compute) scales with usage volume and with the complexity of prompts. A model that works well for short prompts may cost 10× more per case when applied to complex long-form inputs. Token cost forecasting requires usage profiling, not just unit price.

A GenAI ROI model that presents a single point estimate without confidence ranges and sensitivity analysis should be treated as aspirational, not credible. The model must show what happens when accuracy is 5% below assumption, when adoption is 30% below plan, and when inference costs are 2× the estimate.
Benefits in a GenAI ROI model fall into five categories. Each must be quantified as an annual £/$ value with a documented methodology and a confidence classification (High / Medium / Low):
Reduction in FTE hours required to process the same volume
FTE hours saved per case × volume × fully-loaded FTE cost
High — measurable from process timing data
Reduction in rework, exception handling, and downstream error cost
Current error rate × correction cost × projected error rate reduction × volume
Medium — error cost is often estimated, not precisely measured
Ability to process more volume with the same headcount
Additional cases processed × contribution margin per case
Medium — depends on demand being available
Increased win rate, upsell rate, or customer lifetime value from better decisions
Attribution model required: AI contribution isolated from other factors
Low — requires controlled trial; difficult to attribute
Reduction in fraud loss, compliance incidents, or regulatory penalties
Expected loss frequency × expected loss severity × projected AI detection rate
Low to Medium — actuarial modelling required; rare events are hard to forecast

For efficiency and error reduction benefits, use process timing data from the value stream instrumentation (if available) or structured time-and-motion studies. Never accept self-reported time estimates from business stakeholders without triangulation — people systematically overestimate how much time a task takes when they know an AI system might replace it.

GenAI costs split into one-off and recurring. The distinction matters because the CFO will amortise one-off costs over the asset life; recurring costs hit the P&L each year. Both must be modelled explicitly:
Prompt engineering iteration (often 3–5× the initial prompt cost); integration testing with live data
Token growth as prompt complexity increases; retry costs when guardrails fail and re-inference is needed
Observability tooling; model drift monitoring; guardrail infrastructure
Time cost of practitioner adoption: 2–4 hours per staff member to learn the new workflow
Labelling costs if ground truth data is not already available; data quality work before training or RAG indexing
Model risk review cadence (typically annual); regulatory re-clearance after model version changes
Retraining cycles; prompt updates when model versions are deprecated by the provider

Every ROI model presented to a CFO or ESC must include a three-scenario sensitivity analysis. The scenarios test the two highest-risk variables for GenAI: adoption rate and AI accuracy. A model where the bear case still yields positive NPV within 24 months is financeable; a model where the bear case yields negative NPV for 3 years is a funding risk.

def sensitivity_analysis(model, scenarios: dict[str, dict]) -> dict:
    results = {}
    base_benefit = model.total_annual_benefit
    base_cost = model.total_annual_cost

    for scenario_name, adjustments in scenarios.items():
        benefit_mult = adjustments.get("benefit_mult", 1.0)
        cost_mult    = adjustments.get("cost_mult", 1.0)
        volume_pct   = adjustments.get("adoption_pct", 1.0)

        adjusted_benefit = base_benefit * benefit_mult * volume_pct
        adjusted_run_cost = base_cost * cost_mult
        net = adjusted_benefit - adjusted_run_cost
        payback = model.go_live_month + (model.total_one_off_cost / (net / 12)) if net > 0 else float("inf")

        results[scenario_name] = {
            "annual_net_benefit": round(net, 0),
            "payback_months": round(payback, 1),
        }
    return results

scenarios = {
    "Bear (50% adoption, 25% cost overrun)": {"benefit_mult": 0.7, "cost_mult": 1.25, "adoption_pct": 0.5},
    "Base (80% adoption, on-budget)":        {"benefit_mult": 1.0, "cost_mult": 1.0,  "adoption_pct": 0.8},
    "Bull (95% adoption, 10% cost underrun)":{"benefit_mult": 1.1, "cost_mult": 0.9,  "adoption_pct": 0.95},
}

results = sensitivity_analysis(model, scenarios)
print(f"{'Scenario':<45} {'Net Benefit/yr':>14}  {'Payback':>9}")
print("-" * 72)
for name, r in results.items():
    pb = f"{r['payback_months']:.1f} mo" if r["payback_months"] != float("inf") else "Never"
    print(f"{name:<45} £{r['annual_net_benefit']:>12,.0f}  {pb:>9}")

# Scenario                                       Net Benefit/yr  Payback
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bear (50% adoption, 25% cost overrun)              £57,500   36.1 mo
# Base (80% adoption, on-budget)                    £163,200   11.4 mo
# Bull (95% adoption, 10% cost underrun)            £202,345    8.6 mo

Agentic use cases have fundamentally different cost structures than assistive (single-turn) use cases because each agent run involves multiple inference calls, tool calls, and potentially long-running state. The inference cost per completed task is not the API cost of one call — it is the sum of all calls in the agent loop, which varies by task complexity:
Grows as agent accumulates context across steps
Common — tool failures trigger replanning loops
Persistent state per workflow instance; accumulates with concurrent users
Human-in-Loop adds staff time per workflow; must be costed as a recurring people cost

When modelling agentic workload cost, run a profiling exercise on 50 representative cases during the pilot phase to establish the actual average token count and tool call count per completed task. Do not use the best-case or median-case — use the 75th-percentile case as the cost basis. The long tail of complex cases often accounts for 40–60% of total inference cost.
Depends on model tier and prompt length; GPT-4 class ~10× GPT-3.5 class
Small models are often sufficient; cost is very low at scale
Context accumulates; tool call overhead adds ~20% to pure inference cost
Highly variable; long-tail cases can be 5–10× median cost
Embedding model + retrieval infrastructure + generation; chunking strategy affects cost significantly

These ranges assume major commercial foundation model APIs as of mid-2025. Fine-tuned open-source models running on owned compute change the cost structure significantly — inference cost drops, but capital cost of GPU infrastructure and engineering maintenance rises. The ROI model must reflect whichever deployment model is planned.
Investment Case Structure for the ESC
The executive sponsor and ESC do not read detailed ROI spreadsheets. The investment case for ESC approval should be structured as a one-page summary with five sections:
— What is the current state, in numbers? (Value stream KPI baseline, error rate, cycle time, cost per case.)
— What will the AI system do? Which AI pattern? (One sentence.)
— What is the one-off build cost and the annual run cost? (Two numbers.)
— What is the annual net benefit in the base case and the bear case? Payback period in both. 3-year NPV in both.
— What must be true for the programme to proceed to Scale? (The accuracy threshold and adoption rate that trigger the full investment.)

The decision gate is the most important element. It converts the investment case from a blank cheque into a staged commitment: 'We invest £120k to build and pilot. If accuracy ≥ 93% and adoption ≥ 75% after 90 days, we proceed to Scale with an additional £80k investment.' This structure is far more palatable to a CFO than 'we need £200k to build something that might work.'

The following table shows a fully worked 3-year financial model for the FNOL auto-triage use case modelled in the code above. This format is what the programme PMO submits to the CFO alongside the investment case:

Year 1 benefits are 50% of Year 2 because: (a) the system goes live in month 4, leaving only 8 months of production operation; and (b) adoption ramps from 40% in months 4–6 to 80% by end of Year 1. Year 2 and Year 3 reflect full-scale operation. Inference cost grows 20% year-on-year to reflect increasing FNOL volume as the business scales.

The cumulative row is the most important. Payback occurs when cumulative crosses zero — in this model, around month 20. Present the payback month prominently in the ESC summary, alongside the 3-year NPV.
Counting full FTE cost instead of marginal cost

— If 3 FTE are redeployed to higher-value work rather than made redundant, the efficiency gain is the value of the higher-value work produced, not the fully-loaded salary of 3 FTE. Counting salaries of retained FTE as a saving produces inflated benefit claims that fall apart under CFO scrutiny.

— A system that goes live in month 6 of a 12-month Year 1 delivers at most 6 months of benefits — and often less due to adoption ramp. ROI models that show a full year of benefits from a mid-year go-live are common and wrong.

— The comparison baseline should be 'what happens if we do nothing for 3 years?', not 'what is the current state?'. If competitors deploy AI and win market share in the meantime, the cost of inaction must appear in the model.

— Training, process redesign, and adoption support are consistently underestimated. A 500-person claims team spending 3 hours on AI tool training represents £67,500 of labour cost at £45/hour — not including the productivity dip during the adoption curve.
Using list price for inference cost

— Foundation model providers offer volume discounts, and prompt engineering often reduces token counts by 30–50%. Model the inference cost at actual usage profile, not at list price per token × peak token count.

— Foundation model providers discontinue older models on 12–24 month cycles. Migration to a new model version is not free — it requires re-testing, prompt adaptation, and often re-labelling. Add a migration cost provision (typically £10–30k per model per deprecation cycle) to the 3-year model.
Non-Financial Benefits and When to Quantify Them

Not every benefit of a GenAI use case translates directly to pounds or dollars. Two categories of non-financial benefit appear frequently in enterprise investment cases and require specific treatment:
Freeing practitioners from repetitive data-entry or classification tasks often improves job satisfaction and reduces attrition. The ROI model should capture this only when:
The current attrition rate in the affected role is measurably high (> 20% annual turnover).
The cost of replacing a leaver is quantifiable (typically 50–200% of annual salary, depending on role and seniority).

There is a reasonable hypothesis that AI assistance specifically reduces the component of the role that drives attrition (e.g., 'claims adjusters cite data entry as their top frustration in exit surveys').

When these three conditions are met, the benefit is: (attrition rate reduction) × (affected headcount) × (replacement cost per leaver). A 5-percentage-point reduction in attrition among 50 claims adjusters, each costing £30k to replace, produces a £75k annual saving. This is a legitimate ROI line item, not a soft benefit.

Consistent AI-driven decision-making produces an audit trail that manual processes typically do not. When the value stream involves regulated decisions (credit approval, insurance underwriting, medical triage), the AI system's audit log can reduce audit cost and demonstrate regulatory compliance more efficiently than manual sampling. This benefit is quantifiable as:
Reduction in internal audit FTE hours spent on manual sampling × FTE cost.
Reduction in external audit scope (if the auditor accepts AI logs as evidence) × external audit fee reduction.
Reduction in regulatory penalties for process inconsistency (estimated from the organisation's historical penalty frequency and severity).

Non-financial benefits should appear in a separate section of the investment case, not mixed into the financial ROI calculation. The CFO will assign them zero weight in the financial approval decision — they belong in the strategic rationale section, not the NPV calculation.
ROI Model Review Cadence and Variance Reporting

The ROI model is not a document produced once and archived after funding approval. It is a living artefact updated at every programme gate and compared to actual results after go-live. The review cadence:
Baseline model finalised; assumptions documented; sensitivity scenarios agreed with CFO
Actuals vs. model compared on accuracy, adoption rate, and inference cost. Variance > 20% on any line triggers a re-forecast.
Full model re-forecast with pilot actuals substituted for estimates. Bear scenario re-set to reflect observed variance range.
Full benefit realisation check: are the value stream metrics moving as predicted? Variance report to ESC.
Re-forecast remaining model years with actuals. Include model maintenance cost actuals. Flag if cumulative NPV has materially deteriorated.

Variance reporting is the accountability mechanism that distinguishes a credible AI programme from one that treats investment cases as marketing documents. When a benefit line is tracking below forecast, the business owner must explain the variance and propose a corrective action — not simply note it in a dashboard. The ESC reviews variances quarterly; the CFO office reviews annually against the original funding case.
Linking the ROI Model to Value Stream Metrics

The ROI model does not exist in isolation — it must connect directly to the Level 1 and Level 2 value stream metrics defined in the use case intake form. Each benefit line in the ROI model must map to a specific metric with a specific baseline and a specific target:
FNOL processing FTE count (L2 Value Stream)
FNOL intake error rate (L2 Value Stream)
13 percentage points × £4.77k cost per error × 10k FNOLs/yr = £62k/yr
3 days × £16k leakage per day reduction (actuarially estimated) = £48k/yr

This linkage serves two purposes. First, it makes the benefit claims falsifiable — after go-live, the actual metric values are measured against the ROI model predictions and the difference is explained. Second, it prevents benefit double-counting — if two benefit lines both claim credit for the same FTE reduction, the linkage table makes this visible.

GenAI ROI models must account for three sources of uncertainty absent from standard software ROI: probabilistic AI accuracy, adoption dependency, and variable inference cost. Point estimates without sensitivity ranges are not credible.

Benefits split into five categories: efficiency, error reduction, throughput increase, revenue uplift, and risk reduction. Each requires a documented quantification methodology and a confidence classification.

Costs split into one-off and recurring. Engineering build, data engineering, and change management are one-off; inference, platform, compliance review, and model maintenance are recurring. Most models underestimate the last two.

Agentic workloads cost more per task than assistive systems because each agent run involves multiple inference calls with growing context. Use 75th-percentile task complexity as the cost basis, not median.

The ESC investment case is a one-page summary covering problem, intervention, investment, expected return (base and bear), and a decision gate that converts the pilot into a staged funding commitment.
